7: ti emoi kai soi Iesou huie tou theou tou hypsistou — what have I to do with you, Jesus, Son of God Most High? — the demonic recognition of Jesus's identity is ironic: the demons know what the disciples struggle to grasp. Ti emoi kai soi is a Semitic idiom (mah li valak) denoting confrontation and distancing: what is there between us? tou hypsistou — of the Most High — the divine title used predominantly in Gentile contexts (cf. Genesis 14:18-20, Daniel 4:24, Acts 16:17); the Gentile-territory encounter uses the universal divine name.
41: talitha koum — Talitha koum — Mark uniquely preserves the Aramaic words of Jesus (Mark 1:41, Mark 5:41, Mark 7:34, Mark 14:36), which gives the narrative the quality of eyewitness immediacy and grounds the tradition in the actual words of Jesus rather than theological paraphrase. Talitha = little girl (Aramaic); koum = arise, stand up. The interpreter's translation follows (to korasion, soi lego, egeire = little girl, I say to you, arise), emphasizing Jesus's sovereign command-authority over death.
1: Kai Elthan eis to peran tes thalasses eis ten chOran tOn GerasenOn — and they came to the other side of the sea, into the region of the Gerasenes — the textual variant is three-way: Gerasenes (GerasenOn), Gadarenes (GadarEnOn), or Gergesenes (GergesEnOn). UBS5/NA28 reads Gerasenes; Matthew 8:28 reads Gadarenes; Origen proposed Gergesenes to avoid the geographical problem (Gerasa is roughly 35 miles southeast of the lake; Gadara is about 6 miles southeast). The difficulty is that neither city is on the lakeshore where the pigs ran into the sea. The geographical imprecision may reflect the use of major city names to designate regions rather than precise locations. The key point is that this is Gentile territory east of the Jordan: the Decapolis.
2: Kai exelthontos autou ek tou ploiou euthys hupEntEsen autO ek tOn mnEmeion anthrOpos en pneumati akathartO — and as he was getting out of the boat, immediately there met him from the tombs a man in an unclean spirit — euthys again; the encounter is immediate, before Jesus has even left the beach. HupantaO (to go/come to meet, encounter) can be hostile (as in military engagement) or simply a meeting on the road. The man comes from the tombs (ek tOn mnEmeion): he lives among the dead, the extreme of social and ritual exclusion. The description that follows (Mark 5:3-5) is the most detailed portrait of severe demonization in the Gospels.
3: Hos ten katoikEsin eichen en tois mnEmasin — who had his dwelling in the tombs — katoikEsis (dwelling, habitation — from katoeO, to settle and dwell in) is the word for permanent residence: the tombs are not a temporary shelter but his home. The tombs (rock-cut burial chambers in the hillside) were outside the inhabited area, ritually impure, and excluded from normal community life. Kai oute halysei ouketi oudeis edynato auton dEsai — and no one could any longer bind him even with a chain — the emphatic negative triple (oute ... ouketi ... oudeis) stresses the total failure of human restraint: every attempt at social control has failed.
4: Dioti pollakis pedais kai halysesin dedesthai kai diespasthai hyp' autou tas halyseias kai tas pedas syntetriphthai — because he had often been bound with shackles and chains, and the chains had been torn apart by him and the shackles broken in pieces — the iterative pollakis (often, repeatedly) and the perfect passive dedesthai (had been bound — the accomplished state of binding that the aorists of tearing undo) create the picture of repeated failed restraint. The verb diespasthai (torn apart, pulled asunder) is a strong word for violent destruction. Kai oudeis ischyen auton damasai — and no one had strength to subdue him — damazO (to tame, subdue — used for wild animals) identifies the condition: he has become ungovernable, beyond human taming.
5: Kai dia pantos nyktos kai hEmEras en tois mnEmasin kai en tois oresin En krazon kai katakopton heauton lithois — and night and day among the tombs and in the hills he was crying out and cutting himself with stones — the perpetual restlessness (dia pantos nyktos kai hEmEras = throughout night and day, without ceasing) and the self-harm (katakopton heauton lithois = cutting himself with stones — katatkopto from kata + kopto, to beat/cut down) is the picture of a human being at the extreme limit of disintegration. No sleep, no community, constant self-destruction. The scene establishes that what Jesus is about to accomplish is the rescue of a person from the most extreme bondage the Gospels depict.
6: IdOn de ton IEsoun apo makrothen edramen kai prosekynEsen autO — and seeing Jesus from a distance, he ran and prostrated himself before him — two verbs in sequence: edramen (he ran — the immediate, urgent movement toward Jesus across the distance) and prosekynEsen (he prostrated, worshiped/did obeisance — the same verb used for genuine worship). The unclean-spirit-controlled man runs toward Jesus and prostrates before him — the same involuntary submission as Mark 3:11 (the spirits fell before him). The demon's movement is compelled rather than chosen; the human being within is being drawn toward the only source of liberation.
8: Elegen gar autO, Exelthe to pneuma to akatharton ek tou anthrOpou — for he had been saying to him: "come out of the man, you impure spirit!" — the gar (for) makes Mark 5:8 explanatory: the cry of Mark 5:7 ("in God's name don't torture me!") was a response to Jesus's prior command. The exorcism command (exelthe ek tou anthrOpou = come out of the man) had already been issued before the name-asking. This means the demon's pleading in Mark 5:7,10 is resistance to a command already given — the unclean spirit is attempting to negotiate or delay, not simply to identify itself. The sequence: command given → demonic resistance → name-inquiry → final expulsion.
9: Kai epErOta auton, Ti onoma soi? kai legei autO, Legiōn onoma moi, hoti polloi esmen — and he asked him: "what is your name?" and he said to him: "Legion is my name, for we are many" — LegEOn (Legion) is a Latin loanword in Greek: the standard Roman military unit of approximately 5,000 infantry plus cavalry and support (varying by period, 4,000-6,000). The self-identification with a Roman military unit is the deepest irony of the pericope: the occupying power's organizational structure has been adopted by the demonic possession as its name. The answer "we are many" (polloi esmen) clarifies that the name describes multitude, not a single demon named Legion. Whether this is meant to indicate the actual number 5,000 or simply extreme multitude is debated.
10: Kai parekAlei auton polla hina mE autous aposteilE exO tes chOras — and he begged him earnestly not to send them out of the area — the territorial instinct of the demonic: exO tes chOras (outside/beyond the region). This detail appears across demonology: the idea that spirits are bound to or prefer specific territories. Luke 8:31 specifies the request not to go into the abyss. The desperation of the pleading (parekAlei = was earnestly begging, the same verb used by Jairus in Mark 5:22) reveals the hierarchy of the encounter: the Legion that no human could subdue is reduced to begging before Jesus.
11: En de ekei pros tO orei agelE choirOn megalE boskomenE — and there was there near the mountain a large herd of pigs grazing — the herd of pigs signals definitively that this is Gentile territory: Jewish communities did not raise pigs (Leviticus 11:7; Deuteronomy 14:8). The pigs grazing on the hillside near the tombs complete the scene's composite of Gentile uncleanness: Gentile territory, tombs (corpse-impurity), unclean animals. Into this landscape of multiple impurities Jesus arrives and acts.
12: Kai parekalesan auton legontes, Pempon hEmas eis tous choirous, hina eis autous eiselthOmen — and they begged him saying: "send us into the pigs so that we may enter them" — the demons negotiate their own disposal: they request the pigs rather than exile from the region. The request is granted (Mark 5:13 epetrepsen autois = he gave them permission). The compliance with the demonic request has generated theological discussion: why does Jesus permit this? The narrative focus is entirely on the outcome (the man's liberation), not on theological justification for the pigs' destruction.
13: Kai exelthonta ta pneumata ta akatharta eisElthon eis tous choirous, kai hOrmEsen hE agelE kata tou krEmnou eis ten thalassan — and the unclean spirits came out and entered into the pigs, and the herd rushed down the steep bank into the sea — hOrmEsen (rushed, charged — of a stampede) is the vivid verb of the herd's headlong rush. Kata tou krEmnou (down the steep bank — krEmnos = a precipice, steep drop) creates the specific geography. The herd of about two thousand (hOs dischilioi) drowns in the sea. The scale of destruction is vast; Mark preserves the number without explanation or mitigation.
14: Kai hoi boskontes autous ephygon kai apEngeilan eis ten polin kai eis tous agrous — and those herding them fled and reported it in the city and in the fields — the flight of the herdsmen is the immediate human response to the catastrophic loss and the inexplicable event. Apanggellō (to report, announce — the herald verb) carries the news to both the urban and rural populations. The news spreads in two directions: the town and the countryside receive the same report. The result in Mark 5:14b-17 will be the community's collective response to Jesus's arrival.
15: Kai erchontai pros ton IEsoun kai theOrousin ton daimoniZomenon kathEmenon himatismenon kai sOphronounta, ton eschEkota ton legiOna — and they came to Jesus and saw the demonized man sitting, clothed and in his right mind, the one who had the legion — the three participial conditions of the healed man are the precise reversals of his former condition: sitting (not running and crying) / clothed (not naked — implied by the contrast, and explicit in Luke 8:27) / in his right mind (sOphronounta = being of sound mind, the same root as sOphrosynE, sound-mindedness/self-control, the virtue of ordered rational thought). Kai ephobEthEsan — and they were afraid — the community's response to healing is fear, as in Mark 4:41.
16: Kai diEgEsanto autois hoi idontes pOs egeneto tO daimoniZomenO kai peri tOn choirOn — and those who had seen it described to them how it had happened to the demon-possessed man and about the pigs — the eyewitness report (hoi idontes = those who had seen) covers both the healing and the pigs. The conjunction "and about the pigs" (kai peri tOn choirOn) puts the man and the herd in the same report — the community's economic concern (the pigs represent significant wealth destroyed) is as prominent as their theological concern. The double report sets up the community's response in Mark 5:17.
17: Kai Erxanto parakalein auton apelthein apo tOn horiOn autOn — and they began to beg him to leave from their region — the community that saw a man freed from Legion begs Jesus to depart. The word parakalein (to beg, implore) used for the Legion's request (Mark 5:10,12) is now used by the human community: they plead with Jesus the way the demons pleaded. The economic loss (2,000 pigs) likely drives the expulsion request; the supernatural power that freed the man and destroyed the herd is unwanted in their territory.
18: Kai embainontos autou eis to ploion parekAlei auton ho daimoniStheis hina met' autou E — and as he was getting into the boat, the formerly demonized man begged him that he might be with him — hina met' autou E (to be with him) echoes the founding purpose of the Twelve: hina Osin met' autou (Mark 3:14, to be with him). The healed man asks for the same relationship that Jesus constituted in the apostolic appointment. This is the only person in Mark who begs to follow Jesus and is sent away. The contrast with the Twelve who were called but needed persuasion (and the two who left everything immediately) is notable.
19: Kai ouk aphEken auton, alla legei autO, Hypage eis ton oikon sou pros tous sous kai apAggeilon autois hosa ho kyrios soi pepoiEken kai ElEEsen se — and he did not permit him, but said to him: "go to your house, to your own people, and report to them how much the Lord has done for you and how he had mercy on you" — the commission: ho kyrios (the Lord) refers to God in the phrasing but Mark 5:20 will replace it with "Jesus" (hosa epoiEsen autO ho IEsous), creating a Lord = Jesus equation by the narrator's substitution. The mission to the Decapolis is the first explicit Gentile mission in Mark: the healed man becomes the missionary to the 10 Hellenistic cities of the region.
20: Kai apElthen kai Erxato kEryssein en te Dekapolei hosa epoiEsen autO ho IEsous kai pantes ethaumazon — and he went away and began to proclaim in the Decapolis how much Jesus had done for him, and all were amazed — DekapolIs (Ten Cities — the league of Hellenistic city-states east of the Jordan including Damascus, Gerasa, Philadelphia, Gadara, and others) is the mission field of the healed Gentile. His proclamation (kEryssein = to herald, the same word as the apostolic preaching commission of Mark 3:14) in all ten cities is the widest reach any single individual's testimony has in Mark's narrative. The amazement (ethaumazon) is the standard response to unexpected divine intervention.
21: Kai diaperasantos tou IEsou en tO ploiO palin eis to peran synEchthE ochlos polys ep' auton — and Jesus having crossed back in the boat to the other side, a large crowd gathered to him — the return crossing from Gentile territory back to Jewish Galilee resumes the central narrative thread. The crowd immediately gathers (synEchthE = gathered together), consistent with the fame established in Mark 1:28,45; Mark 2:1-2; Mark 3:7-8. The lakeside setting continues as the primary public space of the Galilean ministry.
22: Kai erchetai heis tOn archisyagOgOn, onomati Iaeiros — and one of the synagogue rulers came, named Jairus — the naming of a named individual is notable: Mark names relatively few of the people Jesus encounters. ArchisyagOgos (ruler of the synagogue, president of the synagogue community — responsible for maintaining the building, organizing worship, selecting readers and preachers) is a position of social honor and religious authority. This man of the establishment comes to Jesus and falls at his feet (piptei pros tous podas autou), the same posture of desperate supplication as the demonized man of Mark 5:6.
23: Kai parekAlei auton polla legOn hoti to thygatrion mou eschatOs echei — and he begged him earnestly, saying: "my little daughter is at the last gasp" — eschatOs echei (is at the extreme, is at the last gasp — from eschatos, last, extreme) is a medical idiom for terminal condition. Thygatrion (little daughter — diminutive of thygater) is the affectionate parental form. Hina elthOn epithEs auts tas cheiras hina sOthE kai zEsE — in order that coming you might lay your hands on her so that she might be saved and live — the double hina purpose clause: hands-on (the physical healing gesture) → saving and living. SōthE kai zEsE (be saved and live) — both verbs carry their theological weight beyond mere medical healing.
24: Kai apElthen met' autou kai EkolouthEi autO ochlos polys kai synEthliban auton — and he went with him and a large crowd was following him and was pressing/crushing against him — synEthliban (from synthlibO = to press together, crush from all sides — the same root as thlipsis, tribulation/affliction) describes the crowd's physical pressure on Jesus. The crowd that is simultaneously following Jesus to Jairus's house becomes the medium through which the hemorrhaging woman will reach him: hidden within the pressing crowd, she can approach without announcement.
25: Kai gynE tis ousa en rhusei haimatos dOdeka etE — and a certain woman who was in a flow of blood for twelve years — rhusis haimatos (a flow/discharge of blood) refers to chronic abnormal uterine bleeding. Leviticus 15:25-30 classifies such a condition as tum'a (ritual impurity): the woman is continually in the state that renders her unable to participate in community worship and transmits impurity by touch. Twelve years of this condition means twelve years of social-ritual exclusion that mirrors (and the narrative will pair with) Jairus's twelve-year-old daughter. The age of the daughter and the duration of the woman's illness are the same number — the interlocking stories share the twelve.
26: Kai polla pathousa hypo pollOn iatrOn kai dapanEsasa ta par' heautEs panta kai mEden OphElEtheisa alla mallon eis to cheiron elthousa — having suffered much under many physicians and having spent everything she had and not having been helped at all but rather having come to the worse — the triple compound of failed medicine: much suffering (polla pathousa), total impoverishment (dapanEsasa ta par' heautEs panta = having spent all that was beside herself = all her possessions), and deterioration despite treatment (eis to cheiron elthousa = having gone to the worse). Mark's portrait of the medical system's failure is unsparing: both her funds and her condition have been exhausted by human medicine. What twelve years of physicians could not achieve will take a moment of touch.
27: AkOusasa peri tou IEsou, elthousa en tO ochlO opisthEn hEpsato tou himatiou autou — having heard about Jesus, coming in the crowd from behind she touched his garment — the sequence: hearing about Jesus (akOusasa) → coming in the crowd from behind (hidden approach, maintaining anonymity) → touching the garment (hEpsato tou himatiou). The touch is indirect and minimal: not his body but his garment (himation = outer robe/cloak). The approach from behind avoids the confrontation she dreads: she intends to receive healing without announcement, reversing the flow of impurity (she should make those she touches unclean; but she expects that touching him will make her clean).
28: Elegen gar hoti Ean hapsOmai kan tOn himatiOn autou sOthEsomai — for she was saying: "if I touch even his garments I will be saved/healed" — sOthEsomai (I will be saved — the same sOzO verb used for Jairus's daughter in Mark 5:23 and for the healing pronouncement in Mark 5:34). The woman's inner speech (elegen = she was saying, internal monologue) reveals the logic: even the edge of his garment possesses healing power. This is not magic-thinking but the recognition that the divine power resident in Jesus radiates outward. The faith expressed in the attempt is what Jesus will identify as the operative principle (Mark 5:34).
29: Kai euthys exErAnthE hE pEgE tou haimatos autEs — and immediately the source of her blood dried up — euthys; the healing is instant. HE pEgE tou haimatos (the source/spring of the blood — pEgE = spring, source, fountain) is a vivid anatomical image: the flow's origin is dried up (exEranthe = the same root xErainO as the withered hand of Mark 3:1). Kai egnO tO sOmati hoti iatai apo tes mastigos — and she knew in her body that she had been healed from the affliction — egnO tO sOmati (knew in/by her body — the body as the organ of physical self-knowledge) is the immediate proprioceptive awareness. Mastix (whip, affliction — used in 3:10 for the crowd's diseases) is the word for her condition: a scourge that has been removed.
30: Kai euthys ho IEsous epignous en heautO tEn ex autou dynamin exelElythuian — and immediately Jesus, having known in himself that power had gone out from him — epiginOskO en heautO (having known in himself — immediate internal perception, the same kind of spiritual knowledge as Mark 2:8) perceives the dynamin exelElythuian (power having gone out — perfect active participle of exerchomai, the power has departed and remains absent). The power goes out without Jesus's conscious direction: the healing is initiated by the woman's faith and Jesus's latent healing-power, not by an explicit command. This is unique in the healing narratives. Epistrapheis en tO ochlO elegen, Tis mou hEpsato tOn himatiOn? — turning in the crowd he said: "who touched my garments?" — the question is genuine, not rhetorical.
31: Kai elegon autO hoi mathEtai autou, BlepEis ton ochlon synthlibonta se kai legeis, Tis mou hEpsato? — and his disciples said to him: "you see the crowd pressing in on you and you say 'who touched me?'" — the disciples' incredulity is reasonable: in a crushing crowd, the question seems absurd. Their response reveals they have not grasped the distinction between the crowd's incidental jostling (synthlibO) and the intentional, faith-driven touch. Jesus distinguishes between contact and touch — the whole crowd is in contact but only one person has touched in the theologically significant sense.
32: Kai periblepeto idein ten touto poiEsasan — and he was looking around to see the one who had done this — periblepeto (was looking around — imperfect, sustained scanning action) is the characteristic Markan gaze of Jesus (Mark 3:5,34; Mark 9:8; Mark 10:23). He scans the crowd for the one person in it whose contact was different in kind. The woman knows she is the subject of the search (Mark 5:33: egnOsa hoti ho gegonen en autE = having known what had happened to her).
33: HE de gynE phobEtheisa kai tremousa, eidyia ho gegonen autE, Elthen kai prosepesen autO kai eipen autO pasan ten alEtheian — but the woman, fearing and trembling, knowing what had happened to her, came and fell before him and told him the whole truth — phobEtheisa kai tremousa (fearing and trembling — the two fear-words combined) is the physical response to the exposure of what she has done: touched a rabbi while ritually impure, without permission. Eipen autO pasan ten alEtheian (told him the whole truth) — the public confession before the crowd reverses the anonymous concealment of the approach. The healing sought in secret is now proclaimed openly.
34: Ho de eipen autE, Thygater, hE pistis sou sesOken se — and he said to her: "Daughter, your faith has saved you" — the same teknon/thygater (child/daughter) address of affection as in Mark 2:5, now directed at a woman rather than a man. HE pistis sou sesOken se (your faith has saved you — perfect tense: has saved and the salvation remains) is the Christological statement about the operative cause: not the garment, not the residual power, but her faith. The faith was what connected the woman to the healing power; the power was already present. Hypage eis eirEnEn — go into peace — eis eirEnEn (into peace — the directional preposition, not merely "in peace") sends her forward into a state of wholeness. Kai isthi hygiEs apo tEs mastigos sou — and be well from your affliction — the present imperative isthi (be, continue to be) is the ongoing state of health now confirmed.
35: Eti autou lalountos erchontai apo tou archisyagOgou legontes hoti hE thygatEr sou apethanen, ti eti skylleis ton didaskalon? — while he was still speaking, they came from the synagogue ruler's house saying: "your daughter has died; why do you still trouble the teacher?" — the interruption arrives mid-speech: eti autou lalountos (while he was still speaking). The messengers' message contains both a death-announcement (apethanen = she has died) and a dismissal of Jesus's relevance (ti eti skylleis ton didaskalon? = why do you still trouble/disturb the teacher?). Skylleis (to trouble, harass, flay — a strong word) implies that further involvement of Jesus is now pointless, even burdensome. The death converts the request from healing to impossible resurrection.
36: Ho de IEsous parakoussas ton logon laloumenon legei tO archisyagOgO, ME phobou, monon pisteue — but Jesus, having overheard/ignored the word being spoken, said to the synagogue ruler: "do not fear, only believe" — parakoussas (having overheard — the verb can mean to hear and disregard or to hear accidentally). The ambiguity is productive: Jesus may have heard the messenger's report and chosen to disregard the implied finality of death; or he overhears it while addressing Jairus. Either way, he responds not to the death-news but with the command: mE phobou, monon pisteue (stop fearing, only believe) — the same pattern as the stilling of the storm (Mark 4:40: ti deiloi este, oupO echete pistin?). Faith is the appropriate response to what Jesus is about to do; fear is the inappropriate one.
37: Kai ouk aphEken oudena met' autou synakolouthEsai ei mE ton Petron kai Iakōbon kai IOannEn ton adelphon Iakōbou — and he did not allow anyone to follow with him except Peter and James and John the brother of James — the inner circle of three (Petros kai Iakōbos kai IOannEs) who are the only witnesses to the Transfiguration (Mark 9:2) and the Gethsemane prayer (Mark 14:33). Their designation as witnesses to the resurrection of Jairus's daughter is the first instance of the three-apostle inner circle. The exclusion of the wider crowd is both practical (crowd management) and theological (the miracle requires the selective witness of those whose testimony will be shaped by repeated extraordinary experiences).
38: Kai erchetai eis ton oikon tou archisyagOgou kai theOrei thorybion pollEn kai klaiOntais kai alalAZontas — and he came to the house of the synagogue ruler and observed a great commotion, and people weeping and wailing loudly — thorybos (commotion, tumult) is the noise of a household in mourning. KlaionteS (weeping — the ordinary grief-word) and alalAZontas (wailing loudly — from alalagmos, the battle cry or ritual lamentation shout) describe the professional mourners hired for the occasion. Jewish mourning practice included hired mourners, flutes, and loud lamentation that would have begun immediately upon death. The house is already in full mourning mode.
39: Kai eiselthOn legei autois, Ti thorybEIsthe kai klaiEte? to paidion ouk apethanen alla katheudei — and entering he said to them: "why do you make commotion and weep? the child has not died but is sleeping" — to paidion ouk apethanen alla katheudei (the child has not died but sleeps) is the Christological statement whose ambiguity is intentional: is Jesus correcting their diagnosis (she appears dead but is only in a coma-like sleep)? Or is he using "sleep" as a euphemism for death, indicating that from his perspective the boundary between sleep and death is crossable? The mourners' laughter (kategelOn autou = they ridiculed him) shows they took it as a denial of an obvious fact — she was dead and they knew it. The text supports the latter interpretation: the command egeirai (arise!) in Mark 5:41 treats her as needing resurrection, not awakening.
40: Kai kategelOn autou — and they ridiculed/laughed at him — katagelAO (to laugh down at, deride, mock) is the contemptuous response of those who know what death is and find Jesus's statement absurd. Their laughter is the Christological irony: those who mock will witness the resurrection. HO de ekbalOn pantas — but having cast out all — ekbalOn (having cast out — the exorcism verb!) is used for the dismissal of the crowd: the same word used for casting out demons is used for Jesus removing the mockers from the resurrection scene. The removal is forceful and purposeful; the resurrection happens before a small circle of witnesses, not an audience of skeptics.
42: Kai euthys anestE to korasion kai periepatei En gar etOn dOdeka — and immediately the girl stood up and began to walk, for she was twelve years old — euthys anestE (immediately she stood up — the resurrection verb anistEmi in the aorist) and periepatei (began to walk — imperfect, sustained action). The age specification (En gar etOn dOdeka = she was twelve years old) closes the narrative-numerical bracket: the hemorrhaging woman's 12-year affliction / the girl's 12 years of life. Both the twelve years are healed in the same encounter. Kai exestEsan ekstasei megalE — and they were amazed with a great amazement — the cognate construction (existEmi + ekstasis) for maximal astonishment.
43: Kai diesteilato autois polla hina mEdeis gnO touto — and he gave them many strict orders that no one should know this — the most impossible Messianic Secret command in Mark: the girl who was dead is now alive and walking, and Jesus commands that no one know. The command is clearly unrealistic given the circumstances (the household in mourning, the neighbors, the professional mourners just sent out). The Messianic Secret is a theological principle rather than a sociological strategy: it marks the resurrection as not yet the time for public Christological disclosure. Kai eipen dothEnai autE phagein — and he said to give her something to eat — the physical need following restoration to life is the most tender and human note: she is alive and needs food. The command grounds the miraculous in the practical.
